sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: At a neuromuscular junction preparation, researchers measured end-plate potentials (EPPs) after motor neuron stimulation. Curare reduced EPP amplitude without changing presynaptic calcium influx. An acetylcholinesterase inhibitor increased EPP duration and amplitude. When both drugs were added, EPPs partially recovered but remained below control. Direct electrical stimulation of the muscle fiber still produced contraction in all conditions.
questions.stem: Curare most likely decreases EPP amplitude by blocking:

choices.A: Voltage-gated calcium channels in the muscle T-tubule

explanations.C: Correct. Curare is consistent with a competitive blocker at nicotinic ACh receptors because presynaptic calcium influx is unchanged while postsynaptic response falls.
explanations.A: Presynaptic calcium influx was unchanged, and T-tubule calcium channels are not the site of EPP generation.
explanations.B: Blocking acetylcholinesterase increased EPPs in the experiment.
explanations.D: Direct muscle stimulation still caused contraction, arguing against a primary myosin ATPase block.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. End-plate potentials under drug conditions.

questions.stem: Why does the acetylcholinesterase inhibitor partially reverse curare in the combined condition?

choices.A: It increases the time acetylcholine remains available to compete for receptors
choices.B: It prevents calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
choices.C: It opens voltage-gated sodium channels directly
choices.D: It removes nicotinic receptors from the membrane

explanations.A: Correct. By slowing ACh breakdown, the inhibitor raises effective ACh availability, allowing more receptor activation despite curare.
explanations.B: Preventing SR calcium release would impair contraction and does not explain EPP recovery.
explanations.C: The drug affects ACh breakdown, not direct sodium-channel opening.
explanations.D: Removing receptors would further reduce EPPs.

questions.stem: Direct stimulation producing contraction in all conditions indicates that:

choices.D: The muscle contractile apparatus remained functional
choices.A: Motor neurons could not release acetylcholine
choices.C: Acetylcholinesterase was absent from the preparation

explanations.D: Correct. Direct electrical stimulation bypasses synaptic receptor activation, so contraction shows the downstream muscle machinery can still function.
explanations.A: The experiment does not show failure of ACh release; curare acts postsynaptically.
explanations.B: Curare did not increase contraction; it lowered EPPs.
explanations.C: AChE activity is implied because inhibiting it changed EPPs.

questions.stem: The receptor blocked by curare is best classified as:

explanations.B: Correct. Nicotinic acetylcholine receptors are ligand-gated cation channels that depolarize the motor end plate.
explanations.A: Nuclear hormone receptors bind lipid-soluble hormones and regulate transcription.
explanations.C: RTKs include insulin and growth factor receptors, not nicotinic ACh receptors.
explanations.D: Muscarinic receptors are GPCRs, but the NMJ skeletal muscle receptor is nicotinic.

questions.stem: Which event normally follows sufficient end-plate depolarization in skeletal muscle?

choices.C: Opening of voltage-gated Na+ channels in the muscle membrane
choices.A: Immediate transcription of myosin heavy chain genes
choices.B: Inhibition of tropomyosin removal from actin
choices.D: Export of Ca2+ from the sarcoplasmic reticulum into blood

explanations.C: Correct. A sufficiently large EPP triggers muscle action potentials by opening voltage-gated Na+ channels.
explanations.A: Contraction is rapid and does not require immediate gene transcription.
explanations.B: Ca2+ binding to troponin moves tropomyosin away from actin-binding sites.
explanations.D: SR Ca2+ is released into the cytosol, not exported into blood.

questions.passage: Researchers compared conduction velocity in myelinated and unmyelinated axons of varying diameter. Myelinated axons conducted impulses much faster at every diameter. A sodium-channel blocker eliminated action potential propagation in both axon types, whereas a demyelinating treatment reduced velocity and increased the likelihood of conduction failure without changing resting membrane potential.
questions.stem: What feature explains the faster conduction in myelinated axons?

choices.D: Saltatory conduction between nodes of Ranvier
choices.B: Absence of voltage-gated sodium channels at nodes
choices.C: Use of ligand-gated channels instead of action potentials

explanations.D: Correct. Myelin increases membrane resistance and lowers capacitance, allowing depolarization to jump between nodes of Ranvier.
explanations.A: Myelin increases, not lowers, membrane resistance.
explanations.B: Nodes are enriched in voltage-gated sodium channels.
explanations.C: Axons propagate action potentials; ligand-gated channels are not the replacement mechanism.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Conduction velocity by axon diameter and myelination.

questions.stem: The sodium-channel blocker eliminates propagation because voltage-gated Na+ channels are required for:

choices.A: The rapid depolarizing phase of the action potential
choices.B: Neurotransmitter degradation in the synaptic cleft
choices.C: The resting potential established only by chloride

explanations.A: Correct. Voltage-gated Na+ influx produces the rapid upstroke needed for action potential propagation.
explanations.B: Neurotransmitter degradation is handled by enzymes or transporters, not voltage-gated Na+ channels.
explanations.C: Resting potential depends strongly on K+ gradients and permeability.
explanations.D: Na+ channels are not the machinery of oxidative phosphorylation.

choices.B: Increasing current leak and effective membrane capacitance along the axon
choices.A: Increasing saltatory jump distance without loss
choices.C: Increasing the number of mitochondria in each node
choices.D: Making the resting membrane potential more positive in all cells

explanations.B: Correct. Loss of myelin increases leak and capacitance, slowing depolarization and promoting conduction failure.
explanations.A: Demyelination disrupts saltatory conduction rather than improving jump distance.
explanations.C: Mitochondrial number is not the primary determinant in this experiment.
explanations.D: The passage says resting potential did not change.

questions.stem: Which change would most likely increase conduction velocity in an unmyelinated axon?

explanations.C: Correct. Larger axon diameter lowers internal resistance and increases conduction velocity, as shown in the figure.
explanations.B: Removing extracellular sodium reduces the driving force for depolarization.
explanations.D: More leak would slow conduction and impair propagation.

questions.stem: Failure of propagation with normal resting potential after demyelination suggests the main defect is in:

choices.A: Dynamic spread of depolarizing current during action potential conduction
choices.C: Setting the potassium equilibrium potential at rest
choices.D: Translation of myelin basic protein in muscle cells

explanations.A: Correct. The resting potential can remain normal while impaired insulation prevents sufficient depolarization from reaching the next node.
explanations.B: The axon conduction experiment is not about ACh vesicle synthesis.
explanations.C: Resting potential is stated to be unchanged.
explanations.D: Myelin is produced by glia, not muscle cells.
